title: 10 best herbal teas for colds
intro_desc: Herbal teas for colds are infusions made from various dried herbs, flowers, and spices that can help alleviate the symptoms of a common cold. These teas can soothe a sore throat, reduce congestion, and promote relaxation, making it easier for individuals to rest and recover. Examples of herbal teas that help with colds include chamomile, peppermint, ginger, and echinacea. For instance, chamomile tea can help promote sleep and soothe a sore throat, while ginger tea can reduce inflammation and alleviate congestion. By drinking these teas, individuals can find relief from their symptoms and improve their overall quality of life during a cold.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Yarrow teas helps with colds because of its potent antimicrobial properties, which work to combat the underlying infection that causes congestion and discomfort. The tea's active compounds, such as azulene and chamazulene, help to reduce inflammation in the nasal passages and sinuses, making it easier to breathe and clear mucus from the body. Additionally, yarrow's expectorant properties can help to loosen and eliminate excess mucus, providing relief from congestion and coughs.
remedy_properties: Antibacterial and anti-inflammatory compounds: Yarrow tea's flavonoids, sesquiterpenes, and other compounds have antibacterial and anti-inflammatory effects, helping to combat bacterial infections and reduce congestion and swelling in the nasal passages and sinuses.

Expectorant properties: The mucilage present in yarrow tea helps to loosen and clear out mucus and phlegm from the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel and relieve coughing and congestion symptoms associated with colds.

Antiviral activity: Yarrow tea's flavonoids and other compounds have been shown to exhibit antiviral properties against various viruses, including those that cause the common cold, helping to inhibit viral replication and reduce the severity of symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Used in traditional medicine to treat respiratory issues, including colds, due to their expectorant and anti-inflammatory properties.
Flowers: Contain flavonoids and terpenes that help to ease congestion, reduce inflammation, and soothe a sore throat.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried yarrow leaves and stems of approximately one ounce by weight.
Chop the harvested material into small pieces using scissors or a herb cutter for easier brewing.
Combine the chopped yarrow with 8 ounces of boiling water in a teapot or heat-resistant cup.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes to release the active compounds and flavors.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a cup and discard the solids.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Marshmallow teas helps with colds because of its soothing properties that ease congestion and coughing. The mucilages present in marshmallow root coat and protect the throat and chest, reducing inflammation and discomfort. As a natural expectorant, it loosens mucus, allowing for easier removal from the body, thereby reducing the duration and severity of cold symptoms. Additionally, its anti-inflammatory compounds help to alleviate sore throats and nasal congestion, providing relief from the discomfort associated with common colds.
remedy_properties: Mucilage: Marshmallow root contains a thick, protective mucilage that helps soothe and protect the mucous membranes in the throat and chest, reducing inflammation and discomfort associated with colds.
Anti-inflammatory compounds: Marshmallow tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds such as flavonoids and phenolic acids, which help reduce swelling and alleviate congestion caused by colds.

Expectorant properties: The saponins present in marshmallow root have natural expectorant properties, helping to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs and airways, making it easier to expel phlegm and relieve coughing fits.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots contain mucilages, which help soothe and calm irritated throats and coughs associated with colds.
Seeds: The seeds have anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce congestion and alleviate sinus pressure.
remedy_recipe: Harvest althaea officinalis leaves and roots in late summer or early fall for maximum potency and quality.
Dry the harvested material using a food dehydrator set at 90°f for 12 hours or until crispy brittle texture achieved.
Combine one teaspoon of dried marshmallow root with 8 ounces of boiling water and steep for 5-7 minutes to release flavonoids.
Strain the tea mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large pitcher or teapot, discarding solids.
Cool the tea to room temperature and store in the refrigerator for up to 3 days before serving.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Wormwood teas helps with colds because it contains a compound called thujone, which has natural antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties. When brewed into a tea, wormwood can help soothe a sore throat and reduce congestion by thinning out mucus, making it easier to expel. Additionally, its expectorant qualities can help loosen and clear out excess phlegm, providing quick relief from a stuffy nose and chest.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Wormwood contains flavonoids and terpenoids, which reduce inflammation in the body, helping to alleviate cold-related symptoms such as congestion and sinus pressure.
Antiviral activity: Wormwood has been shown to possess antiviral properties, which can help combat the underlying viral infections that cause the common cold, reducing its severity and duration.

Mucus-thinning properties: The essential oils in wormwood tea have a natural expectorant effect, loosening and thinning mucus in the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel and helping to relieve congestion.
remedy_parts: Roots: Containing bitter compounds like sesquiterpene lactones, which help to reduce inflammation and open up airways, making them a popular choice for treating respiratory issues like coughs and congestion.
Seeds: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory agents, wormwood seeds are used to create teas that soothe sore throats, calm coughs, and ease cold symptoms.
Bark: With its high concentration of alkaloids and flavonoids, wormwood bark is often employed to make teas that reduce fever, relieve chest congestion, and combat bronchitis.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried wormwood leaves and flowers in late summer or early fall for optimal flavor and potency.
Rinse the harvested wormwood material with cold water to remove any dirt or debris then chop into small pieces.
Combine 1 teaspoon of chopped wormwood with 1 cup of boiling water in a heat-resistant tea infuser or strainer.
Allow the mixture to steep for 10-15 minutes before straining and discarding the solids while retaining the liquid tea.
Strain the tea into a cup and sweeten if desired with honey or sugar then enjoy within 24 hours of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Cinnamon teas helps with colds because it contains cinnamaldehyde, a compound that has natural antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. When consumed as a warm tea, cinnamon can help to ease congestion by thinning mucus and making it easier to expel, while also reducing the severity of coughing and sneezing. Additionally, cinnamon's antibacterial properties can help to combat the underlying infection that causes the cold, promoting faster recovery and relief from symptoms.
remedy_properties: Antiviral properties: Cinnamon's antiviral properties can help combat the viral infection that causes a cold, reducing its severity and duration.
Anti-inflammatory properties: Cinnamon's anti-inflammatory compounds can help reduce congestion, sinus pressure, and sore throat discomfort associated with colds.
Expectorant properties: Cinnamon's expectorant qualities can help loosen and clear mucus from the chest and sinuses, making it easier to breathe and cough up excess phlegm.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: Cinnamon rhyzomes have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, making them effective in soothing sore throats and relieving congestion caused by colds.
Bark: The bark of the cinnamon plant contains a compound called cinnamaldehyde, which has natural decongestant properties that help to ease respiratory issues associated with colds.
remedy_recipe: Measure one teaspoon of dried cinnamomum verum bark for every 8 ounces water.
Heat fresh filtered water to boiling point and pour over the measured bark.
Steep the mixture for five to seven minutes or longer to desired strength.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Add honey or sugar to taste if desired and enjoy warm or chilled.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eureka teas helps with colds because it contains a blend of natural herbs that work together to soothe and calm the body's symptoms. The peppermint and eucalyptus in the tea help to clear congestion and ease a sore throat, while the ginger and lemon balm reduce inflammation and combat fatigue. As a result, Eureka teas provide relief from cold and flu symptoms, helping to promote a faster recovery and get you back to feeling your best.
remedy_properties: Antiviral compounds: Herbal Eureka teas, such as echinacea and sage, contain antiviral compounds that help to reduce the severity and duration of cold symptoms by inhibiting the replication of viruses.

Anti-inflammatory agents: Teas like peppermint and eucalyptus contain anti-inflammatory agents that help to reduce congestion, coughing, and sinus pressure by reducing swelling in the nasal passages and sinuses.

Expectorant properties: Herbs like thyme and lemon balm have expectorant properties that help to loosen and clear mucus from the lungs and airways, making it easier to breathe and cough up excess mucus.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are used because they contain anti-inflammatory compounds that help relieve congestion and soothe a sore throat.
Bark: The bark is utilized due to its ability to reduce fever and ease coughing, making it an effective ingredient in tea blends for cold relief.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh eureka citrus leaves from the tree or purchase dried eureka peel online.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried eureka peel or four to six sprigs of fresh leaves per cup.
Combine measured eureka peel or leaves with 200 milliliters of boiling filtered water in a tea infuser or teapot.
Steep the mixture for seven to nine minutes, allowing the citrus oils to infuse into the hot water.
Strain the tea and serve immediately, garnished with a slice of lemon or a sprig of fresh mint if desired.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Eastern purple coneflower teas helps with colds because it contains antiviral compounds that target the viruses responsible for causing respiratory infections. The tea's active ingredients, such as echinacein and sesquiterpenes, have been shown to inhibit the replication of cold-causing viruses, reducing their ability to spread and multiply in the body. This helps to alleviate symptoms like congestion, coughing, and sore throats, allowing the immune system to fight off the infection more effectively.
remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: The tea contains antioxidants that help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the body, which can contribute to cold symptoms such as congestion and coughing.
Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids and phenolic acids present in the tea have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help alleviate symptoms of a cold such as sore throats and sinus pressure.
Expectorant properties: The mucilage present in the tea helps loosen and clear mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to expel and relieve congestion and coughing associated with colds.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots of the Eastern Purple Coneflower contain antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds that help reduce congestion and soothe a sore throat.
Bark: The bark of the Eastern Purple Coneflower is rich in tannins, which helps to reduce inflammation and fight off infections, making it effective against cold symptoms.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried echinacea purpurea flowers in the morning for optimal potency.
Measure two teaspoons of dried flowers or one tablespoon of fresh petals into a heat-resistant teapot.
Pour boiling water over the herbs and let it steep for 5-7 minutes to release the active compounds.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove any remaining plant material.
Serve the tea hot, sweetened with honey if desired, and drink within 24 hours of preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Siberian ginseng teas helps with colds because of its adaptogenic properties, which enable it to enhance the body's natural defense mechanisms. It increases the production of white blood cells, such as neutrophils and lymphocytes, that help fight off infections, while also boosting the immune system's ability to respond to viral invasions. Additionally, Siberian ginseng has anti-inflammatory compounds that help reduce congestion and alleviate symptoms like sore throats and runny noses, providing relief from cold-related discomfort.
remedy_properties: Adaptogenic properties: Siberian Ginseng tea helps the body adapt to stress and promotes resistance to infection, making it easier for the immune system to fight off cold-causing viruses.

Antioxidant activity: The antioxidants present in Siberian Ginseng tea help protect the body from oxidative damage caused by free radicals, which can exacerbate cold symptoms like congestion and inflammation.

Immune-stimulating compounds: Siberian Ginseng contains bioactive compounds that stimulate immune cell function, increasing production of white blood cells and activating natural killer cells to combat invading pathogens and reduce the severity of cold symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in ginsenosides, which help to boost immune system and alleviate symptoms of colds such as fatigue and body aches.
Rhyzomes: Contains bioactive compounds that help to reduce inflammation and relieve congestion associated with colds.
remedy_recipe: Gather dried eleutherococcus senticosus root and bring water to boil.
Measure out one teaspoon of dried herb for every eight ounces of boiling water.
Steep the herb in the boiling water for seven to ten minutes.
Strain the tea into a cup and discard the solids.
Enjoy the tea hot or iced within two hours after preparation.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Horsetail teas helps with colds because it contains high levels of silicates, which have been shown to help reduce inflammation and congestion in the body. Silicates also work to loosen and clear mucus from the respiratory tract, making it easier to breathe and cough up excess phlegm. Additionally, horsetail's anti-inflammatory properties may help to soothe a sore throat and reduce sinus pressure, providing relief from cold symptoms.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory compounds: Horsetail tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like silica and flavonoids, which can help reduce inflammation in the nasal passages and sinuses, relieving congestion and discomfort caused by colds.

Antiviral properties: Horsetail has been shown to have antiviral activity against certain viruses, including those that cause respiratory tract infections like the common cold. This property helps to combat the infection and reduce its severity.

Mucilage content: Horsetail tea contains a thick, gel-like substance called mucilage, which can help soothe and protect the mucous membranes in the throat and lungs, reducing irritation and discomfort caused by coughing and congestion.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and silica, roots help to soothe respiratory issues and reduce inflammation associated with colds.
Stems: Stems contain a high concentration of flavonoids, which have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate congestion and coughing.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried horsetail herb equisetum arvense, weighing about 2-3 tablespoons for each serving.
Rinse the harvested horsetail herb gently with cool water to remove any debris or dirt.
Combine 2-3 tablespoons of the rinsed horsetail herb with 1 liter of boiling water in a large pot.
Steep the mixture for 10-15 minutes, allowing the flavors and nutrients to infuse into the liquid.
Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a teapot or cup, discarding the solids immediately.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Tasmanian blue gum teas helps with colds because it contains a unique blend of antioxidants, anti-inflammatory compounds, and antimicrobial properties that work together to soothe and calm the throat and nasal passages. The tea's rich source of vitamin C and flavonoids also boosts the immune system, helping to fight off the infection and reduce congestion. Additionally, the tea's expectorant properties help to loosen and clear mucus from the chest, making it an effective natural remedy for relieving cold symptoms.
remedy_properties: Eucalyptol: A natural expectorant, eucalyptol helps to loosen and clear mucus from the chest and nasal passages, making it easier to breathe and expel excess phlegm.
Catechins: These antioxidants have anti-inflammatory properties that may help reduce congestion and sinus pressure by decreasing swelling in the nasal passages and sinuses.
Menthol: A natural decongestant and analgesic, menthol helps to ease coughs, soothe a sore throat, and relieve nasal congestion, making it easier to breathe and swallow.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in antioxidants and anti-inflammatory compounds, Tasmanian Blue Gum roots help to reduce congestion and soothe sore throats.
Rhyzomes: Known for their antimicrobial properties, Tasmanian Blue Gum rhyzomes work to combat infection-causing bacteria and viruses, relieving symptoms of colds.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh eucalyptus globulus leaves, approximately 2 tablespoons per cup of tea.
Rinse the harvested leaves thoroughly with clean water to remove any dirt or debris.
Combine the rinsed leaves with 1 cup of boiling water in a teapot or infuser.
Allow the mixture to steep for 5-7 minutes before straining and serving.
Store leftover tea in an airtight container in the refrigerator for up to 3 days.

remedies_list.remedy_desc: Licorice teas helps with colds because it contains compounds that have natural anti-inflammatory properties, which can help soothe a sore throat and reduce congestion. The tea's flavonoids and glycosides also work to thin mucus, making it easier to expel, while its expectorant properties help loosen and clear out excess phlegm. Additionally, the tea's antimicrobial properties can help combat the underlying infection that is causing the cold symptoms.
remedy_properties: Anti-inflammatory properties: Licorice root contains anti-inflammatory compounds like glycyrrhizin, which help reduce inflammation in the body and alleviate symptoms such as sore throats and congestion.
Mucolytic properties: The saponins present in licorice root have mucolytic effects, breaking down mucus and making it easier to expel, relieving congestion and coughing associated with colds.

Antioxidant properties: Licorice tea is rich in antioxidants, which help protect the body from oxidative stress and inflammation caused by viral infections like the common cold, reducing the severity of symptoms.
remedy_parts: Roots: The roots are the most commonly used part of the licorice plant in herbal medicine, as they contain a high concentration of glycyrrhizin, which has anti-inflammatory and antiviral properties that help relieve respiratory congestion and soothe sore throats.

Bark: The bark is used to make teas for colds because it contains flavonoids, saponins, and other compounds that have antiseptic and anti-inflammatory properties, helping to reduce fever and alleviate symptoms of the common cold.
remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh or dried glycyrrhiza glabra roots weighing about one ounce per quart water.
Chop the harvested roots into small pieces to ensure optimal extraction of flavors and properties.
Combine the chopped roots with one quart of boiling water in a large pot, steeping for 10-15 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a separate container, discarding solids.
Store the liquid tea in an airtight container at room temperature for up to three days, serving hot or chilled.

supplementary_best_treatment: The best way to treat colds with herbal teas is by choosing teas with antiviral and anti-inflammatory properties, such as echinacea, ginger, and elderberry. These teas can help soothe a sore throat, reduce congestion, and boost the immune system to fight off the cold virus. Drinking warm tea can also help alleviate symptoms by increasing hydration and relieving nasal congestion. For best results, drink at least 2-3 cups of herbal tea daily at the first sign of a cold. However, it's important to note that while herbal teas can help manage cold symptoms, they should not be used as a substitute for medical treatment.
supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to colds that are treated with herbal teas include sore throats, coughs, and congestion. Peppermint tea can soothe a sore throat and act as a natural decongestant. Ginger tea is known to alleviate nausea and reduce inflammation, often caused by a common cold. Chamomile tea has antimicrobial properties and can help with relaxation, promoting better sleep. Licorice root tea can also soothe a sore throat and reduce inflammation, while thyme tea has been shown to loosen mucus and relieve coughs. However, it's important to consult with a healthcare professional before starting any herbal tea regimen, especially for pregnant women, children, and individuals with underlying health conditions.
